title: Build a green-wood shaving horse
subtitle: The English bodger pattern in oak and ash
excerpt: Build a shaving horse in the English bodger pattern from green oak and ash. The foot-pedalled bench clamps a workpiece for two-handed drawknife and spokeshave work. An advanced project: the first piece of dedicated green-woodwork furniture, made with the tools it enables.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for the bodger bench design. Newey & Drage Practical Carpentry for the mortise-and-tenon joints and the dumb-head clamp mechanics. The English bodger pattern is documented in the Museum of English Rural Life open-access archive on woodland crafts.

glossaryTerms.definition: A foot-pedalled bench that clamps a workpiece for two-handed drawknife and spokeshave work.
Freshly felled or recently riven wood, still high in moisture content. Carves easily under hand tools.
Split along the grain with a froe and maul rather than sawn.
A two-handled blade pulled toward the body along the workpiece.

projectSchedule.title: Rive and rough the bench components
projectSchedule.body: Split the log, rough-shape the bench beam and legs.

projectSchedule.title: Assemble the bench body
projectSchedule.body: Bore the leg holes and fit the legs into the beam.

projectSchedule.title: Fit the dumb-head and footrest
projectSchedule.body: Shape and pivot the dumb-head clamp. Fit the footrest crossbar.

projectSchedule.title: Joints have locked
projectSchedule.body: Six weeks drying. The dry-tenon-in-dry-seat joints have tightened. The bench is ready for heavy use.

content.text: The
shaving horse
is the workhorse bench for all
green-woodwork
: drawknife shaping, spokeshave finishing, and any two-handed tool that needs the work held without a vice. Building one from
riven

oak and ash using the tools it is about to enable is the natural introduction to the tradition. The English bodger pattern has a low angled beam on four legs, a pivoting dumb-head clamp, and a foot-pedal bar.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
Materials

You need a green oak or ash log at least 180 cm long and 25 cm in diameter for the beam. Four legs in ash (the same riven-billet method as the stool-leg tutorial), each 55 cm long and 35 mm in diameter. The dumb-head clamp is a single piece of ash or oak 50 cm long and 8 cm across. The footrest is a straight rod of ash or hazel 60 cm long and 35 mm in diameter.
Making the beam

Rive a section from the log to give a beam roughly 180 cm long and 15 cm wide and 10 cm deep. The beam has a flat working top surface. Drawknife the faces flat and use the spokeshave to refine the top to a smooth platform.

Bore four leg holes through the beam at 15 degrees from vertical (splay angle). The two front legs angle forward; the two rear legs angle back. Use the brace and bit with a 35 mm bit. The leg splay keeps the bench stable under the lateral forces of drawknife work.
Fitting the legs

Shape the top of each leg as a round tenon 35 mm in diameter and 40 mm long. The green tenon should fit slightly tight in the bored hole (35 mm). Drive each leg in from below until it protrudes 5 mm above the beam top. Saw or pare the protruding stump flush. As the legs dry, the tenon shrinks and the mortise locks the joint permanently.
The dumb-head clamp

The dumb-head is an H-shaped piece: a crossbar at the top that presses down on the workpiece, and two parallel legs that pass through slots in the beam. The legs pivot in the slots, which transfers foot-pedal pressure into clamping force at the top.

Cut two parallel slots through the front of the beam 10 cm apart. Each slot is 25 mm wide and 150 mm long, running from the forward end of the beam at 45 degrees down toward the underside. The dumb-head legs ride in these slots.

Shape the dumb-head crossbar to press 30 mm above the beam top when at rest. When the foot pedal drives the legs forward and down, the crossbar drops and clamps any workpiece placed between the crossbar face and the beam surface.
The foot pedal

A round footrest bar 60 cm long passes through the lower ends of the dumb-head legs. The rider sits on the beam with both feet on this bar. Pressing forward on the bar drives the clamp down. Releasing allows the workpiece to be repositioned.
Finishing
The bench does not need finishing for workshop use. Apply raw linseed oil to the top working surface once dry to resist moisture.
Variations
Shorter bench for seated-on-the-floor use: reduce the beam to 130 cm and the leg length to 35 cm. Common in Japanese green-woodwork.
Single-leg dumb-head: a simpler construction with one central pivot leg rather than two parallel ones. Less stable under sideways workpiece loads but easier to make.

rows.symptom: Clamp releases under lateral drawknife pressure
rows.cause: Dumb-head leg slots angled too steeply, reducing the mechanical advantage
rows.fix: Reduce the slot angle to 35 degrees from vertical. A shallower angle gives more clamping force per unit of foot pressure.

rows.cause: Leg splay angles not matched, or one leg dried and shrank unevenly
rows.fix: Saw the short leg slightly longer to restore level, or shim the foot with a thin oak wedge.
